speaker: interviewer
text: December 2nd, 1994. The survivor's name is Mermelstein, Mel. That's Mel Mermelstein. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Interviewer is Renee Firestone. The city, Huntington Beach. The language, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And besides, we're not saying-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] David is Mel Mermelstein's youngest son. We asked him to join us before we even started the testimony, because he had to leave. So thank you, David, for staying with us for a minute. Would you like to tell us, how does it feel-- how did it feel-- to be growing up with a father whose background was so tragic and who lived with it, probably, the rest of his life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's been interesting. I mean, for me, I was-- when I was growing up and I became aware of the Holocaust and everything involved with it, my dad was involved in a case, a lawsuit, where the neo-Nazis were saying it didn't happen. So that was really-- it kind of put us in the limelight. We were in the news a lot, and that-- that was kind of my experience. It was always-- since my dad was in the spotlight, I was in the spotlight with him, and it was-- it was a big part of my growing up, the Holocaust was. And it was-- was good, because I-- I got to have a voice for my friends, who otherwise would know nothing of it. I mean, where we grew up, it's-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you grow up in a Jewish atmosphere and a Jewish background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me and probably three-- you know, I had three other friends who were Jewish. And most, you know, just knew nothing of the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it must've been even harder in the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But they were really receptive. I mean, if I ever spoke of the Holocaust, everyone really listened, I would say. And was very interested, because of who my dad was. Everybody has tremendous respect for my dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your siblings, your brother, sisters, how did they accept that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they-- they accepted it well. It was hard for all us, but-- but everybody's, you know, pretty much dealt with the Holocaust the best we can. It's not always the funnest subject to deal with, but it's important to pass on. It's important to let everybody know, because there's a lot ignorance.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it mean to you for the future, for your future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's-- it baffles me, because a lot of the younger kids, the skinheads and whatnot, these idiots, it's-- it's-- they need to be taught a lesson that, listen. It's about death. It's about murder. It's about-- hate goes beyond just simple words, you know? And the Holocaust proves what can happen when hate is out of control. And I think if you expose people, and they see exactly what went on, maybe it'll curve their-- their thoughts and their words, and curve their actions. Because right now, I see a lot of these skinheads heading towards violence and this kind of stuff. You've really got to-- got to tell them what happened, show what the Holocaust was all about, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I understand that you put all this in words, and you put it on paper. Would you like to tell us your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I-- I went with my father to the Holocaust Memorial in Washington, DC. And on the way back, I just wrote a poem. I was a creative writing major and had a poetry class, so I've been writing quite a few poems. It's called 'Flight Home From the Memorial,' written on the plane. 'Crammed in so tight, I could chew on the neck in front of me. I rest my head on a cushion. My swollen stomach folds over my belt as my toes grow cold. In retrospect, it was a good trip. Old questions dim by quiet explanation-- the loving voice of a father, licking his own wounds as he bandages mine. Images put forth so violent and full of hate. Crazy madness scorches my chest while I fight back the tears of a memory never seen. My nostrils fill with the dank smell of death, but I have to breathe. We continue to move, uncomfortably sliding through a silent crowd. There are no rules here for a survivor and a son. Eyes pass over our name tags. We are ghosts in a place with no boundaries, only grim details of a people's bloody slaughter. My eyes burns as I watch my father climb into the corner of a box car. He kneels down, and is eaten by his shadow. 'This is where I sat,' he says, in a childlike voice. I can't hold back. A tear rolls off my nose. 'Your grandmother, grandfather, aunts, and uncles.' He points to the opposing corner. I begin to shake. My throat tightens. I turn my head. This is very much a part of me, I think, as my mind goes blank.'

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mel, what is your message to a wonderful young man like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that number one is to be true to himself, and that-- to be realistic. He has no choice but to begin to be realistic that, one of these days, you know, I'll-- I'll have to leave, and that I would like him to carry on, if possible without him and his family being injured by it. Because the deal with this barbaric event is not a subject that I would recommend anyone as a steady diet. Life has to go on. And at the same time, you must never forget what they did to us. And that is all I ask him. No more, no less.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank both of you for this little conversation. It means a lot to me. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry that we couldn't get your son at the end of the tape, but we are very appreciative of him joining us in the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please state your name, where, and when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Mel is the first name. Mermelstein is my last name. And I was born in Mukacevo. Of course, at a time I was born, September the 25th, 1926, it belonged to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Therefore, whenever I'm asked where I'm from, I have a problem sort of clarifying it, even, because very few people can understand, you know, the movements-- the boundary movements in those days. And they really moved rapidly. See? So in 1938, of course, we were taken over by the Hungarian fascists, see? So-- I also need the name of your father, your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sisters and brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My father's name was Bernat, B-E-R-N-A-T. And my mother's name was Fany. My father was born in 1899. My mother was born in 1900. And my older sister, she was 20 years old, and her name was Etu, E-T-U. Then I had a brother who was-- was born on 1925, and his name was Lajos, L-A-J-O-S. And then my kid sister, whose name was Magda-- Magda. She was born in 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a winemaker. He worked for a winery. He started, as a matter of fact, working for them way before I was even born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a-- a homemaker. She had her hands full, of course-- you know, four kids. But at the same time, she did have a-- a trade. She was a seamstress. And periodically, she would sew dresses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I do remember her clientele, her customers, very many of them were-- were actresses, you know? But that did not prevent her from sewing dresses and other things, coats, and-- and-- and whatever, you know, for peasants, even.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember that vividly, that she did that. And she was even-- I think that she was well paid for it, too, because she was very well known as a good seamstress.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of schooling do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started going to Czech school in 1932, at the age of six. And then I went through fifth grade, in 1938. And then after the Hungarian switch, I began to go to Hungarian school. But soon after-- I would say when I was about 14 or 15-- 14, I would say-- my father-- well, I had no choice, because I couldn't really attend any higher education. So I became an apprentice, a machinist's apprentice. But as an apprentice, I also attended school for literature, and reading, and writing, and what have you. And that was up until 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would say there's no such thing as, in Mukacevo not to be religious. We were not the type that is the ultra orthodox, ultra religious. However, we lived by the laws of Israel. There was-- there was no such thing as-- just to give you an example-- as eating meat, and then try to eat something-- you know, milk, you know, or whatever. And another thing is, of course, when Friday eve came, you know, that's it. The-- the week ended, and the Sabbath began. And then Saturday night, after sundown, work, the week began. And Sunday was the first day of work. So we lived by the laws of Israel no different than Jews are living today in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father a Zionist?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not to the extent that I or the kid did, my sister and myself. We were in a Zionist movement. We were brought up in a Zionist movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what movement was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a Hashomer Hatzair. But don't ask me what it was then, and I'm going to go into politics. That was what I belonged, Hashomer Hatzair. However-- Hatzair. My sister was a Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us the difference between the two?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Betar was-- was revisionist. They were-- they were fighters. And Hashomer Hatzair were more of the field workers. More of the-- I understood it very well. As a matter of fact, my sister was supposed to have left for Palestine at that time, in 1938 and early '39. But it was very difficult to get away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was still-- the gymnasiums were still open. She went to Hebrew gymnasium, and she was still able to-- to study Hebrew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Other than that, she didn't work. My older sister, she was more of a sort of, you know, company and assisting whatever needed to be done at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1938, Hungary occupied the territory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What changed after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after that, it was nothing to-- you know, to sort of be happy about, because very shortly after, the Hungarian fascists took over. The racial laws almost immediately took effect. For instance, if a Jew-- like, in this case, my father was very affected by it, because working for a Jew who owned a winery, that winery was nationalized, taken away from the Jew, handed over to a non-Jew. And so my father was without a job, practically, with one exception-- that he was such an expert in his field that with all those problems, for all those anti-Jewish laws, my father was retained by the non-Jew who took over that business. The only-- the only thing they asked him to do is not wear a cap, as he always did. He had to go bare head. Plus, he had to change his name from Mermelstein to Molnar-- Molnar, which is a Hungarian name. You know, that was asinine, if I may put it that way, because they knew him. They knew him. My father was very well known in that-- in Mukacevo. And yet, whenever they came in to buy wine, or liquor, or beer, it was referred to him as Mr. Molnar instead of Mr. Mermelstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this legally changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, definitely not. Oh, no. As a matter of fact, legally, we were supposed to have applied for Hungarian citizenship. I remember that. Now, that my father did do for the protection of the family, because there were rumors going around in those days already that if you're not a citizen of-- if you don't have Hungarian citizenship, you are subject to deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did these anti-Jewish laws effect you, the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very much. Number one, my father, as I said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was still at an age-- in 1939, he was 40 years old, and he was drafted into a labor battalion. And they dragged them off for about three, four, five, or six months to work, and we were completely left out-- kids, four kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we lived, actually, by the grace of God, if I may put it that way. Plus, my mother, as a seamstress, was able to-- to sew for some of the peasants and-- and get-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and get things for it instead of money-- food. You know? What other things changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: What other things changed was that we were in constant fear. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was-- we were in constant fear. However, later on, in 1944, there was a tremendous change, that we were ordered to wear, first, yellow armbands, which later turned into the yellow star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course then, shortly after, we were rounded up and then taken into brick fields for confinement. You know? Of course for the sole reason, no other, than just being Jewish. Did you know this was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you see, that-- we're jumping from '38, '39, to 1944, which I can understand. So I have to preface it by simply saying that, in March, and I believe it was the 23rd, of 1944, Nazi SS troops suddenly appeared on the scene. And-- and we didn't know exactly what it was all about. But once the Nazi troops were in there, things have changed drastically, tremendously. While we had problems, fear of the Hungarian soldiers, we began to fear these Germans to no end. Because I know, as an apprentice, I did have a little bit of experience-- a little-- what-- another apprentice who was of German nationality, a Sharabian, who, once we had an encounter, you know, and he always Jew-baited and was always referring to me, you dirty Jew, and this and that. And then one day, we fought, and he ripped my shirt off, and whatever. And he says, you Jew, you. And I-- of course, I didn't just sit idly by. I-- I hit him back. And he said, you Jew. They're going to gas you, just like they're gassing them in Poland. And that shocked me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, what's he talking about? Hans, I figured, what's this all about? And sure enough, this was a Friday, and on a Sunday, I decided to go to his neighborhood, to the Sharabian neighborhood. And who do I see? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What's his name-- Hans, in a beer parlour, with the young SS soldiers, 16, 17, 18-year-olds. When I finally arrived on Auschwitz and we got to know what was happening, what was taking place in Auschwitz-- gassing-- I tied the two together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took you to the brick factories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to the brick field. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had no-- we had no roofs over our heads, and there were some bricks in different areas. And they did order us to somehow fabricate, or whatever, little huts. And that's where we were. We were put into that hut. And whoever was able to gather bricks, and build a hut, and put a roof over it somehow, was-- was protected. So I was there with my whole family, with the exception of my older brother. My brother decided to go to Mukacevo, to the city instead of-- he actually fooled the-- those who came together us.

speaker: interviewee
text: He walked away and went across the bridge and went to Mukacevo to help my grandfather, and my aunts, and my uncle, and-- and also his cousins, you know? Little kids. And what we did there was actually just wait-- wait. And we were-- Wait for what?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were brutalized. We were waiting for, perhaps, the Russians to arrive, nothing else. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was no work there, actually, you know? They only gathered us there for a purpose, and I-- none of us knew exactly what the purpose was, other than what we were told that this is-- where we are right now is a war zone, and we are going to take you to a safety zone. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did not feed us. The city from-- you know, the Mukacevo, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the population-- the Jewish population in Mukacevo would ship in food into the brick field, and we would cook just soup. That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: One month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, we were told to-- to gather our things and get ready to be shipped to Germany, is what we were told, to a camp that is better suited than this place. It was all, you know-- you know, false, all, you know, stories that we were told, given. Tried to-- at the same time, we were-- we were not-- you know, we were brutalized to a great extent, primarily by the climate. And also by the conditions where we were, that-- there, for instance, you-- to give you an example, for instance, a boy like me of 17, to observe a father whom I always respected and even had awe-- a certain respectful awe of some sort, you know, that he is great, and he will always be able to, you know, protect us, and all that. And I saw him [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a sort of way-- helpless. He couldn't do anything. As much as-- and I looked at a number of other people like him, and they were completely and totally helpless. If you-- if-- you know, there was-- they were caged in. And quite a number of older men in particular who were in Hungarian labor battalions in the Ukraine and came back, they had an idea what was being done to the Polish Jews and to the Ukrainian Jews. And it isn't to say that they knew and that they had an answer as to what to do about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- it's one of the things that is so difficult to even explain. And I looked at my father, and I begin to act bravely, even to show him, father, don't worry. I'll be all right. Because I saw in him this constant, you know, fear of what is going to happen to us. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was unable to do anything to rectify the situation. The only thing I remember him to say is when we were ordered to pack up, and we'll be going in two transports. One of them will leave the 18th of May, 1944, and the other one, the 19th of May.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came over to us, and he said, let's go on the second transport, hoping that perhaps the second transport will never leave here. See? That the Russians that are not too far will come and somehow save us.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were always-- I mean, we were not communists, if you will. You know, this has nothing to do with politics. But we looked upon the Red Army as heroes, saviors. Come and get us. And we heard the shots in the backgrounds, all the big guns going off. We heard of the-- of the partisans, you know, Ukrainians and Russian partisans who cut away from the Nazis and the Hungarians, who were in the woods fighting the Nazis and the Hungarians, and did not come to save us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened is that a row of boxcars were-- were spotted on the railroad-- railway line. And as soon as that took place, as a young-- as a young fellow, I began to put two and two together. I says, now why in heaven would they bring us to a brick field? And there were other brick fields in the near area that they took us to. And when I saw those-- those boxcars, that gave me a sort of answer. I was able to put two and two together. They brought us over here because they had a plan to take us from here like a herd of cattle. That's it. That's the only place in Mukacevo where you could spot railway boxcars. And that's what they did. They spotted the boxcars, and the first transport went off. And I remember-- I remember seeing my-- my aunts and my uncles going off with their bundles. And it was hot, I remember, the 18th of May. It was hot and there was no roads there-- dusty. And they went, and some of them were just waving goodbye, I'll see you later, like, you know, see you tomorrow. But then tomorrow came for us. They spotted additional boxcars, and we went in. You could say you wouldn't go in, but you had no choice. You did go in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the conditions in the boxcars?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is indescribable, that I know. Because I don't even know how they were able to put in so many humans. There were the kids, especially, and then there were some bundles there too. We were practically sitting on top of each other, is what it was, squeezed together. And frankly, I don't even remember. See, we went-- we got into the boxcar the 19th. Rode all day, all night. Then the 20th, rode all day and all night. Finally arrived, late in the afternoon, the 21st of May, which, of course, we discovered to be Auschwitz. Never heard of Auschwitz before.
